forecasts.reasoning: The historical baseline shows Artemis Accord signatories are added frequently, with approximately a dozen or more nations joining per year in the 2023-2024 period. Signings tend to cluster around major international space diplomacy events. The UN Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (COPUOS) main session typically occurs in June (e.g., June 2024, May/June 2023, June 2022), serving as a common venue or forcing function for space agreements. Given the accelerating base rate of 6-8 months per year seeing at least one signing, and June's status as a high-activity month for space diplomacy, the probability of a signing in June is higher than average. While recent signings in April and May might suggest a temporary exhaustion of the pipeline, the US continues to actively recruit partners from a large pool of non-signatory nations. Therefore, I estimate a 68% chance that at least one country will sign the Accords in June 2025.

John Fetterman has explicitly stated he will not resign, and his personal brand is built on defying his political critics and being stubborn in the face of left-wing or intra-party backlash. While the background articles from mid-May 2025 suggest he is isolated and angry amidst "article blowback," making a drastic move like formally leaving the Democratic caucus (and thereby risking committee assignments, which requires planning and negotiation with Republicans) or resigning within the roughly 30 days before the July 1 deadline is unlikely. Defections and resignations typically happen over a longer timeline even in the face of pressure. Therefore, despite the apparent rift, the extremely short time horizon and his refusal to resign heavily weigh against him ceasing to caucus with Democrats before July 1.
